Title: Nitric Oxide in Plants: Production and Cross-talk with Ca2+ Signaling

Common_Genes.Applied_Species_Latin_Name: Arabidopsis thaliana; Nicotiana tabacum; Nicotiana plumbaginifolia
Common_Genes.Applied_Species_Variety: Xanthi for Nicotiana tabacum; not reported for Arabidopsis and N. plumbaginifolia
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Manipulating nitrate reductase can alter nitric oxide generation linked to nitrogen assimilation, ABA signaling, and hypoxia responses, but engineering use requires caution because NR deficiency also lowers nitrite and L-arginine pools and may confound NO-related phenotypes. Deployment should consider nitrate supply and anion-channel activity.
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes nitric oxide production from nitrite in plants; major enzymatic NO source in some contexts such as ABA signaling and hypoxia.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Biochemical assays showing nitrite-to-NO conversion in vitro and in vivo; genetic evidence from NR-deficient mutants; pharmacological comparisons; NO measurements with DAF-2DA.
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: ABA signaling; hypoxia; nitrate/nitrite availability; ammonium-grown nitrate-deprived conditions
Common_Genes.Gene_Accession_Number: NIA1; NIA2 loci not specified in this paper
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: NR normally reduces nitrate to nitrite for nitrogen assimilation, while NO-forming activity is about 1% of nitrate-reducing activity. In nia1 nia2 mutants, leaf L-arginine is strongly reduced, complicating interpretation of NR-versus-NOS pathway studies.
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: NR deficiency perturbs nitrogen metabolism and substrate pools, potentially causing broad metabolic and signaling defects beyond NO reduction.
Common_Genes.Source_Species_Variety: Xanthi for tobacco; not reported for Arabidopsis
Common_Genes.Subcellular_Localization: Cytosol; also plasma membrane-associated NR activity discussed for apoplast-related NO production
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. NR is a key nitrogen-assimilation enzyme that also reduces nitrite to NO. 2. Genetic and biochemical evidence support NR as a major enzymatic NO source in some plant signaling contexts. 3. Interpretation of NR-deficient mutants must consider altered nitrite and L-arginine pools.
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Guard cells; tobacco cell suspensions; roots/apoplast depending on context
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Signaling in ABA responses, hypoxia adaptation, defense signaling, Ca2+ signaling cross-talk

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species_Variety: Xanthi for tobacco; not reported for Arabidopsis
Pathway_Genes.Biosynthetic_Pathway: Nitric oxide biosynthesis from nitrite within nitrogen/NO metabolism
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful target to tune NO-linked signaling through nitrogen metabolism, but should be engineered with nitrogen-use traits and substrate balance in mind; stacking with transport/channel components may be needed for predictable outcomes.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Reduces nitrite to nitric oxide using reducing power; canonical activity reduces nitrate to nitrite using NAD(P)H.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Increases NO formation from nitrite and supports NO-dependent signaling under ABA signaling and hypoxia.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: In vitro enzyme assays; in vivo NO detection; NR-deficient mutant analysis; physiological treatments
Pathway_Genes.EC_Number: 1.7.1.1 for nitrate reductase; NO-forming side activity not separately assigned
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: ABA; hypoxia; nitrate/nitrite supply; ammonium-grown nitrate-free conditions
Pathway_Genes.Gene_Accession_Number: NIA1; NIA2 loci not specified in this paper
Pathway_Genes.Metabolic_Step_Position: Side-route from nitrite; branch point connecting nitrate assimilation to NO production
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: NO-generating activity is minor relative to nitrate reduction. In some signaling contexts, absence of NO in nitrate-deficient cells may also reflect impaired nitrate efflux/Ca2+ signaling rather than substrate shortage alone.
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Nitrite-to-NO branch of nitrate assimilation/NO metabolism
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Changes can disrupt nitrogen assimilation and global metabolism.
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Important enzymatic NO source in certain contexts, but not universally established as sole or rate-limiting NO source
Pathway_Genes.Source_Species_Variety: Xanthi for tobacco; not reported for Arabidopsis
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. NR converts nitrite to NO in addition to its canonical nitrate-reducing role. 2. Genetic and biochemical data indicate it is a major NO source during ABA signaling and hypoxia. 3. Its contribution is context dependent and confounded by nutrient status and nitrate-channel signaling.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Guard cells; tobacco suspension cells; roots/apoplast
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Defense signaling, ABA signaling, hypoxia response, Ca2+-linked signaling

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential target for rhizosphere/apoplast NO engineering and root signaling, but identity is still unknown, preventing direct breeding use.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Reduces apoplastic nitrite to nitric oxide at the plasma membrane.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Catalyzes NO production in tobacco roots from apoplastic nitrite.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Biochemical activity assays in root plasma membrane fractions
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Molecular identity remains unknown. Activity may coordinate with plasma membrane-bound NR reducing nitrate to nitrite.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. A tobacco root plasma membrane activity converts nitrite to NO. 2. This step likely contributes to apoplastic NO production. 3. The responsible protein has not yet been identified.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Local signaling in roots and stress/defense-related responses

Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Not a direct metabolic enzyme target for nutrient fortification, but could be used to modulate NO-associated signaling and defense/stress traits. Engineering should be cautious because it may primarily affect mitochondrial function rather than a dedicated NO-synthesis step.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Associated with positive regulation of NO accumulation; mutants show reduced NO levels, but direct NOS catalytic role is disputed.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Mutant analysis (Atnos1/noa1), subcellular localization, physiological treatments with ABA/LPS/pathogens, NO measurements
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Condition-dependent controller associated with NO accumulation, but not proven as a direct NO synthase.
Regulation_Genes.Gene_Accession_Number: AtNOA1/AtNOS1 locus not specified in this paper
Regulation_Genes.Metabolic_Process_Controlled: NO production indirectly associated with mitochondrial function
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Initially proposed as a novel NOS-like enzyme producing NO from L-arginine in mitochondria, later reinterpreted as a GTPase involved in mitochondrial biogenesis. Reduced NO in mutants may be secondary to mitochondrial defects.
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Manipulation may impact mitochondrial biogenesis and broader growth/energy traits.
Regulation_Genes.Regulation_Mode: Indirect positive association with NO accumulation
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. AtNOA1 was first described as a NOS-like protein linked to arginine-dependent NO production. 2. Mutants show reduced NO under ABA, LPS, and pathogen treatments. 3. Later evidence suggests it is more likely a mitochondrial GTPase affecting NO indirectly through mitochondrial biogenesis.
Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Guard cells and other tissues under stress/signaling conditions

Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential signaling node to reprogram NO-responsive stress pathways, especially osmotic/salt contexts, but not yet directly tied to metabolite biofortification. Use should consider broad stress-network effects.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Activated by NO and required in NO-linked hyperosmotic stress signaling; proposed downstream mediator of NO effects.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: In-gel kinase assay after DEA/NO treatment; pharmacological NO scavenging; stress-response analysis
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Condition-dependent controller transmitting NO signals rather than determining NO synthesis itself.
Regulation_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: DEA/NO treatment; hyperosmotic stress; salt stress
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Not reported; activity induced by osmotic/salt stress and NO
Regulation_Genes.Metabolic_Process_Controlled: NO-responsive stress signaling and possibly Ca2+-linked signal transduction
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: A 42-kDa SnRK2 kinase activated by NO; activation is insensitive to La3+ and intracellular Ca2+ channel inhibitors, suggesting a pathway distinct from SIPK activation.
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Likely broad effects on abiotic stress signaling and growth responses if perturbed.
Regulation_Genes.Regulation_Mode: Protein phosphorylation activation in NO signaling
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. NO rapidly activates the 42-kDa kinase NtOSAK. 2. NtOSAK is a SnRK2 family member linked to osmotic and salt stress signaling. 3. It may mediate some NO effects independently of the La3+-sensitive Ca2+ influx route.
Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Tobacco suspension cells under NO donor or stress treatment

Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Could be targeted to modulate NO/Ca2+/defense signal integration, but likely with pleiotropic effects on pathogen and stress responses.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Activated by NO and acts as a Ca2+-dependent MAPK mediator in NO signaling.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: In-gel kinase assay; phospho-MAPK immunoblot; anti-SIPK immunoprecipitation; inhibitor analysis with La3+ and ruthenium red
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Important downstream NO signal integrator rather than a biosynthetic determinant; links NO to defense/stress signaling outputs.
Regulation_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: DEA/NO treatment; pathogen/elicitor and osmotic stress contexts; Ca2+ influx blockade by La3+
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Activity induced by NO, pathogens, and osmotic stress
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Activity induced by NO, pathogens, and osmotic stress
Regulation_Genes.Metabolic_Process_Controlled: NO-triggered defense/stress signaling downstream of Ca2+ influx
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The NO-induced 48-kDa MAPK was identified as SIPK. Activation depends on La3+-sensitive plasma membrane Ca2+ influx but not on RYR inhibition. SA dependence appears context specific.
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Pleiotropic defense and stress phenotypes likely if manipulated.
Regulation_Genes.Protein_Complex_Involvement: MAPK cascade implied but complex members not identified here
Regulation_Genes.Regulation_Mode: Phosphorylation activation downstream of NO and Ca2+ influx
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. NO activates a 48-kDa MAPK identified as SIPK. 2. SIPK activation requires La3+-sensitive Ca2+ influx. 3. SIPK is part of NO-dependent stress and defense signal transduction.
Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Tobacco suspension cells under NO-related signaling conditions
Regulation_Genes.Upstream_Signals_or_Inputs: NO; Ca2+ influx; pathogens; osmotic stress

Regulation_Genes.Applied_Species_Variety: dnd1 mutant background mentioned; wild type not specified
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential target to alter Ca2+-dependent NO production in guard-cell and immune signaling, but likely to affect defense, HR, and channel-mediated ion fluxes broadly.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Required for Ca2+-dependent NOS-like NO production in response to LPS; loss of function abolishes inward Ca2+ currents and NO production.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Mutant analysis in dnd1 (nonfunctional CNGC2); electrophysiology/inward Ca2+ current evidence; NO response to LPS; SNP rescue of HR phenotype
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Essential upstream activator of Ca2+-dependent NO synthesis in the tested guard-cell immune context.
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Guard cells in immune signaling context
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Guard cells in immune signaling context
Regulation_Genes.Gene_Accession_Number: CNGC2 / DND1 locus not specified in this paper
Regulation_Genes.Metabolic_Process_Controlled: Ca2+ influx required for NOS-like NO production
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: CNGC2 functions upstream of NO production rather than downstream. NO donor SNP partially rescues the hypersensitive response defect of dnd1 mutants.
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Manipulation may strongly alter immune responses, cell death regulation, and ion homeostasis.
Regulation_Genes.Primary_Regulatory_Targets: NOS-like NO-producing process; inward plasma membrane Ca2+ current
Regulation_Genes.Protein_Complex_Involvement: Ion channel likely acts as channel complex; partners not identified here
Regulation_Genes.Regulation_Mode: Transport control enabling Ca2+-dependent activation of NO production
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Indirectly enables NO-dependent downstream defense programs
Regulation_Genes.Source_Species_Variety: dnd1 mutant used for loss-of-function evidence
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CNGC2 is required for LPS-induced inward Ca2+ currents in Arabidopsis guard cells. 2. Functional CNGC2 is necessary for Ca2+-dependent NOS-like NO production. 3. Loss of CNGC2 compromises HR, partially rescuable by an NO donor.
